perturbation.original_text: Operator shall comply, in the performance of its duties and responsibilities hereunder, in all respects with all Environmental Laws and all Environmental Permits.
perturbation.changed_text: Operator shall endeavor to comply with Environmental Laws and Environmental Permits, making efforts deemed appropriate by its management.
perturbation.explanation: The phrase "endeavor to comply," combined with "making efforts deemed appropriate by its management," introduces ambiguity. It makes compliance discretionary rather than mandatory and allows Operator's management to decide what level of effort is sufficient. This weakens the obligation to comply with environmental regulations.
perturbation.law_explanation: LA R.S. 30:2011 mandates strict compliance with environmental regulations. The modified text allows Operator to choose their level of compliance, contradicting the mandatory nature of the law. The term “deemed appropriate” renders compliance open to interpretation, which fails to ensure mandatory compliance.
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates strict compliance with environmental laws and permits. The changed text weakens this obligation by stating the operator shall 'endeavor to comply' and make efforts 'deemed appropriate by its management.' This introduces ambiguity and discretion, making compliance dependent on the operator's management's assessment rather than a strict legal requirement. Although no law snippets are provided, the perturbation explanation and law explanation assert that this change contradicts laws mandating strict environmental compliance, creating a legal ambiguity where mandatory action is required.

perturbation.original_text: Notwithstanding the foregoing, all personnel provided by Operator to operate and maintain the Rail Facility shall have the requisite background, training and skill necessary to operate such a facility in accordance with all current industry standards and any and all applicable state and federal Laws, Environmental Laws, and Environmental Permits.
perturbation.changed_text: Notwithstanding the foregoing, all personnel provided by Operator to operate and maintain the Rail Facility should generally possess what Operator considers a suitable background, training, and skill set to possibly operate such a facility, loosely adhering to industry standards and applicable state and federal Laws, Environmental Laws, and Environmental Permits.
perturbation.explanation: This weakens the requirement for qualified personnel. Replacing 'shall have' with 'should generally possess' and 'requisite' with 'what Operator considers suitable' introduces subjectivity. The addition of 'possibly operate' further diminishes the guarantee of competence. 'Loosely adhering' implies a non-strict compliance which contradicts the need to comply with any and all applicable laws and standards.
perturbation.law_explanation: 29 CFR § 1910.120(q)(6) requires specific training and certification for employees involved in hazardous waste operations, including rail facilities that handle hazardous materials. The modified text's ambiguous language ('suitable background,' 'possibly operate,' 'loosely adhering') does not guarantee this level of training and competence, potentially violating OSHA's HAZWOPER standard.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 1910.120 - Hazardous waste operations and emergency response. | Occupational Safety and Health Administration Skip to main content Home By Standard Number 1910.120 - Hazardous waste operations and emergency response. Part Number: 1910 Part Number Title: Occupational Safety and Health Standards Subpart: 1910 Subpart H Subpart Title: Hazardous Materials Standard Number: 1910.120 Title: Hazardous waste operations and emergency response. GPO Source: e-CFR 1910.120(a) Scope, application, and definitions - 1910.120(a)(1) Scope . This section covers the following operations, unless the employer can demonstrate that the operation does not involve employee exposure or the reasonable possibility for employee exposure to safety or health hazards: 1910.120(a)(1)(i) Clean-up operations required by a governmental body, whether Federal, state, local or other involving hazardous substances that are conducted at uncontrolled hazardous waste sites (including, but not limited to, the EPA's National Priority Site List (NPL), state priority site lists, sites recommended for the EPA NPL, and initial investigations of government identified sites which are conducted before the presence or absence of hazardous substances has been ascertained); 1910.120(a)(1)(ii) Corrective actions involving clean-up operations at sites covered by the Resource Conservation and Recovery Act of 1976 (RCRA) as amended (42 U.S.C. 6901 et seq. ); 1910.120(a)(1)(iii) Voluntary clean-up operations at sites recognized by Federal, state, local or other governmental bodies as uncontrolled hazardous waste sites; 1910.120(a)(1)(iv) Operations involving hazardous wastes that are conducted at treatment, storage, and disposal (TSD) facilities regulated by 40 CFR parts 264 and 265 pursuant to RCRA; or by agencies under agreement with U.S.E.P.A. to implement RCRA regulations; and 1910.120(a)(1)(v) Emergency response operations for releases of, or substantial threats of releases of, hazardous substances without regard to the location
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 29 CFR § 1910.120 - Hazardous waste operations and emergency response. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 29 CFR § 1910.120 - Hazardous waste operations and emergency response. CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 1910.120 Hazardous waste operations and emergency response. (a) Scope, application, and definitions —(1) Scope. This section covers the following operations, unless the employer can demonstrate that the operation does not involve employee exposure or the reasonable possibility for employee exposure to safety or health hazards: (i) Clean-up operations required by a governmental body, whether Federal, state, local or other involving hazardous substances that are conducted at uncontrolled hazardous waste sites (including, but not limited to, the EPA's National Priority Site List (NPL), state priority site lists, sites recommended for the EPA NPL, and initial investigations of government identified sites which are conducted before the presence or absence of hazardous substances has been ascertained); (ii) Corrective actions involving clean-up operations at sites covered by the Resource Conservation and Recovery Act of 1976 (RCRA) as amended ( 42 U.S.C. 6901 et seq. ); (iii) Voluntary clean-up operations at sites recognized by Federal, state, local or other governmental bodies as uncontrolled hazardous waste sites; (iv) Operations involving hazardous wastes that are conducted at treatment, storage, and disposal (TSD) facilities regulated by 40 CFR parts 264 and 265 pursuant to RCRA; or by agencies under agreement with U.S.E.P.A. to implement RCRA regulations; and (v) Emergency response operations for releases of, or substantial threats of releases of, hazardous substances without regard to the location of the hazard. (2) Application. (i) All requirements of part 1910 and part 1926 of title 29 of the Code of Federal Regulations apply pursuant to their terms to hazardous
perturbation.justification:
The modified text introduces ambiguity regarding the qualifications and adherence to standards for personnel operating the Rail Facility. The original text mandates that personnel 'shall have the requisite background, training and skill necessary' and operate 'in accordance with all current industry standards and any and all applicable state and federal Laws'. The changed text weakens this requirement by stating personnel 'should generally possess what Operator considers a suitable background' and 'loosely adhering to industry standards and applicable state and federal Laws'.

The Law Explanation points to 29 CFR § 1910.120(q)(6), which requires specific training and certification for employees involved in hazardous waste operations. While the provided snippets do not explicitly contain section (q)(6), they establish the scope and application of 29 CFR § 1910.120, which broadly covers hazardous waste operations and emergency response. The ambiguity introduced by the modified text fails to ensure the level of training and competence required by the law, potentially violating OSHA's HAZWOPER standard. The snippets define the scope as covering operations involving hazardous wastes that are conducted at treatment, storage, and disposal (TSD) facilities, meaning that if the Rail Facility is such a facility, strict adherence to standards and laws is required, which the modified text undermines with its vague language.

perturbation.original_text: Operator shall promptly pay all direct costs and expenses incurred in operating and maintaining the Rail Facility as they become due, without reimbursement by Owner save and except to the extent specifically provided otherwise herein.
perturbation.changed_text: Operator shall attempt to pay direct costs and expenses incurred in operating and maintaining the Rail Facility in a timely manner, subject to available funds, without reimbursement by Owner except as specifically provided herein.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall promptly pay' to 'shall attempt to pay in a timely manner' and adding 'subject to available funds' weakens the payment obligation. It introduces ambiguity about when and if payments will be made, making the payment obligation contingent and discretionary rather than mandatory.
perturbation.law_explanation: Louisiana Revised Statute 9:2781 addresses the prompt payment of contractors and subcontractors. While the Operator is not strictly a contractor/subcontractor in the construction sense, the underlying principle of prompt payment for services rendered is relevant. Introducing 'subject to available funds' could allow for delayed or non-payment, which goes against the principle of timely compensation for services, potentially leading to legal disputes over payment obligations under Louisiana law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: LA Rev Stat § 9:2781 :: RS 9:2781 — Open accounts; attorney fees; professional fees; open account owed to the state :: 2011 Louisiana Laws :: US Codes and Statutes :: US Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of the Louisiana Laws 2024 Louisiana Laws 2023 Louisiana Laws 2022 Louisiana Laws 2011 Louisiana Laws (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2011 Louisiana Laws Revised Statutes TITLE 9 — Civil code-ancillaries RS 9:2781 — Open accounts; attorney fees; professional fees; open account owed to the state LA Rev Stat § 9:2781 What's This? §2781. Open accounts; attorney fees; professional fees; open account owed to the state A. When any person fails to pay an open account within thirty days after the claimant sends written demand therefor correctly setting forth the amount owed, that person shall be liable to the claimant for reasonable attorney fees for the prosecution and collection of such claim when judgment on the claim is rendered in favor of the claimant. Citation and service of a petition shall be deemed written demand for the purpose of this Section. If the claimant and his attorney have expressly agreed that the debtor shall be liable for the claimant's attorney fees in a fixed or determinable amount, the claimant is entitled to that amount when judgment on the claim is rendered in favor of the claimant. Receipt of written demand by the person is not required. B. If the demand is forwarded to the person by first class mail to his last known address, a copy of the demand shall be introduced as evidence of written demand on the debtor. C. If the demand is made by citation and service of a petition, the person shall be entitled to pay the account without attorney fees by delivering payment to the claimant or the claimant's attorney within ten days after service of the petition in city courts and fifteen days after service of the petition in all other courts. D. For the purposes of this Section and Code of Civil Procedure Articles 1702 and 4916, "open account" incl
perturbation.justification: The modified text changes 'shall promptly pay' to 'shall attempt to pay in a timely manner, subject to available funds,' significantly weakening the payment obligation. This introduces ambiguity about when and if payments will be made. LA Rev Stat § 9:2781 addresses the prompt payment of open accounts. While the Operator may not fit the exact definition of an 'open account' as defined in the statute, the underlying principle of prompt payment is undermined by the added condition of 'subject to available funds,' which potentially allows for delayed or non-payment. The change makes the obligation discretionary, contradicting the legal spirit of ensuring timely compensation.
